voice.speaker: Ryan
voice.instruct: Very fast storyteller tempo, clipped and continuous, racing straight from sentence to sentence with almost no gaps, twice as fast as a calm documentary narrator. Only the final two sentences ease off, settling to a quiet conclusive close.

research.summary: Three outlaws across three centuries built legends on courtesy: Claude Duval (1643-1670), the French highwayman who reputedly never used violence and once took only part of a strongbox after dancing a courante with a victim's wife; Black Bart (Charles E. Boles, c.1829-1888), who robbed Wells Fargo stagecoaches at least 28 times on foot, never fired his shotgun, left two authenticated poems, and was caught by laundry mark F.X.O.7 on a dropped handkerchief; Bill Miner (1846-1913), the Grey Fox, reputed originator of "Hands up!", tied by belief to Canada's first train robbery, captured after a $15 haul, died in custody after his last escape attempt. Fame never spared any of them.
facts: Claude Duval convicted of six robberies 17 Jan 1670; hanged at Tyburn 21 Jan 1670, aged about 27; Charles II refused a pardon
Duval memorial epitaph, St Paul's Covent Garden: "Here lies DuVall: Reder, if male thou art, / Look to thy purse; if female, to thy heart"
Black Bart robbed Wells Fargo stagecoaches at least 28 times, 1875-1883, first and last at Copperopolis; first take $160
Black Bart left two authenticated poems (1877, 1878); was invariably polite, reportedly never fired his shotgun, feared horses, fled on foot
Black Bart traced via laundry mark F.X.O.7 to Ferguson & Bigg's California Laundry; released Jan 1888; said "No, gentlemen. I'm through with crime."; vanished after 28 Feb 1888
Bill Miner: nicknames Grey Fox / Gentleman Bandit; reputed originator of "Hands up!"; Monte Creek robbery netted $15 plus kidney pills
Miner escaped BC Penitentiary 1907, never recaptured in Canada; escaped twice in Georgia; died 2 Sep 1913 of gastritis after drinking brackish water during an escape attempt

The most feared robbers of three centuries shared one rule. Manners.
A highwayman who danced with his victims. A bandit who left poems at the scene. A train robber famous for saying please.
